'''Andrew Dunemann''' (born 6 October 1976) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer and coach who played in the 1990s and 2000s.

Primarily a {{rlp|fe}}, he played for the [[Gold Coast Seagulls]], [[North Queensland Cowboys]], [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] and the [[Canberra Raiders]] in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]], and [[Halifax Blue Sox|Halifax]], [[Leeds Rhinos]] and the [[Salford City Reds]] in the [[Super League]]. After spending most of his career in the halves he spent some time later in his career as a {{rlp|hk}}.

==Background==
Born in [[Brisbane]], Dunemann grew up in [[Toowoomba, Queensland|Toowoomba]], where he played his junior rugby league for the [[Toowoomba Rugby League|Newtown Lions]] and attended [[Harristown State High School]]. In 1992, he started at five-eighth in Harristown's 16–17 [[GIO Schoolboy Cup|Commonwealth Bank Cup]] final loss to [[Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield]], winning the competition's Player of the Tournament award.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/447597/dunemanns-journey-continues-at-knights/|title=Dunemann's journey continues at Knights|date=27 October 2009|website=Newcastle Herald}}</ref>

==Playing career==
In Round 18 of the [[1993 NSWRL season]], at age 16 years and 299 days old, Dunemann made his first grade debut for the [[Gold Coast Seagulls]] in their 16–46 loss to the [[North Sydney Bears]]. This was his only appearance for the Seagulls that season. In 1994, he played six games, starting five at {{rlp|fe}}. In 1995, he became more of a regular in the side playing 13 games, scoring his first try in a 20–27 loss to the [[Newcastle Knights]].

In 1996, Dunemann signed with the [[North Queensland Cowboys]], joining his older brother [[Ian Dunemann|Ian]] at the club. In Round 10 of the [[1996 ARL season]], he made his debut for the club, playing alongside his brother in a 14–38 loss to the [[Sydney City Roosters]]. In doing so, they became the first pair of brothers to play for the club. In his first season at the Cowboys, he played 16 games. In 1997, he played 14 of the club's 18 games, starting the majority at {{rlp|hb}}. In 1998, Dunemann's final season at the club, he played 21 games, scoring three tries.

In 1999, Dunemann joined the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]], playing just five games for the club before making a mid-season move to [[England]] to play for the [[Halifax Blue Sox]] in the [[Super League]]. Over three and a half seasons with the club, he played 74 games, scoring 19 tries.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/story/0,3604,214205,00.html|title=Giants despair over Gibson's late swoop|date=26 April 2000|website=The Guardian}}</ref>

In 2003, Dunemann signed with the [[Leeds Rhinos]], starting at halfback in their [[2003 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]] final loss to the [[Bradford Bulls]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2003/apr/28/rugbyleague.bradfordbulls|title=Leeds Rhinos 20 – 22 Bradford Bulls|date=27 April 2003|website=The Guardian}}</ref> In 2004, he played 24 games for the Rhinos but was left out of their [[2004 Super League Grand Final]] side that defeated Bradford.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/ex-leeds-rhinos-ace-dunemann-still-thrilled-world-title-win-over-canterbury-bulldogs-2860609|title=Ex-Leeds Rhinos ace Dunemann still thrilled by world title win|date=21 May 2020|website=Yorkshire Evening Post}}</ref> In February 2005, he started at {{rlp|hk}} in Leeds' 39–32 [[2005 World Club Challenge|World Club Challenge]] win over the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2005/feb/05/rugbyleague.leedsrhinos|title=Rhinos charge to top of the world|date=4 February 2005|website=The Guardian}}</ref> In August 2005, he came off the bench in their [[2005 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]] final loss to [[Hull F.C.]]<ref>{{cite news|title=Hull stun Leeds in Challenge Cup|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/4169230.stm|access-date=17 August 2013|publisher=[[BBC News]]|date=27 August 2005}}</ref> His last game for the Rhinos was their [[2005 Super League Grand Final|2005 Grand Final]] loss to Bradford, in which he started at {{rlp|hk}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.therhinos.co.uk/2005/03/01/pain-of-rhinos-hooker-andrew/|title=Pain of Rhinos hooker Andrew|date=1 March 2005|website=Leeds Rhinos}}</ref>

In 2006, joined the [[Salford City Reds]], playing 25 games in his lone season at the club before returning to Australia.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.therhinos.co.uk/2005/10/21/salford-sign-dunemann/|title=Salford sign Dunemann|date=21 October 2005|website=Leeds Rhinos}}</ref>

In 2007, Dunemann returned to the NRL after eight years, joining the [[Canberra Raiders]]. He played two games for the club, coming off the bench in both before retiring due to injuries.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.raiders.com.au/about/all-time-first-grade-player-list/|title=All time first grade player list|website=Canberra Raiders}}</ref>

==Coaching career==
In 2008, Dunemann became head coach of the newly established [[Northern Pride RLFC|Northern Pride]] in the [[Queensland Cup]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2008-03-24/pride-too-good-for-dolphins/1081200|title=Pride too good for Dolphins|date=23 March 2008|website=ABC}}</ref> In his [[2008 Northern Pride RLFC season|first season]] in charge, the Pride finished third and came within one game of the [[2008 Queensland Cup#Finals series|Grand Final]]. In [[2009 Northern Pride RLFC season|2009]], the Pride finished second and qualified for the [[2009 Queensland Cup#Finals series|Grand Final]], losing 18–32 to the [[Sunshine Coast Sea Eagles]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.news.com.au/news/rocket-rod-jensen-back-to-fire-up-northern-pride/story-fna7dq6e-1225748354414?sv=8e79cc9ef6077d4d20a1af271991424b|title=Rocket Rod Jensen back to fire up Northern Pride|date=17 August 2007|publisher=News.com.au}}</ref>

In 2010, Dunemann joined the [[Newcastle Knights]], serving as an assistant to head coach [[Rick Stone]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/447597/dunemanns-journey-continues-at-knights/|title=Dunemann's journey continues at Knights|website=Newcastle Herald|date=26 October 2009 }}</ref> In 2012, after two years at Newcastle, he moved to the [[Canberra Raiders]] as an assistant coach of the NRL side and head coach of the club's [[NRL Under-20s|under-20]] side.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2012/02/22/canberra-raiders-2012-preview/|title=Canberra Raiders 2012 preview|date=22 February 2012|publisher=National Rugby League}}</ref> In the [[NRL Under-20s|National Youth Competition]], he led the Raiders to the Grand Final, which they lost to the [[Wests Tigers]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2012/09/26/toyota-cup-grand-final-team-lists/|title=Toyota Cup Grand Final Team Lists|date=26 September 2012|publisher=National Rugby League}}</ref>

In April 2013, Dunemann coached the [[Queensland under-20 rugby league team|Queensland under-20 team]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2013/04/15/under-20s-origin-squads-named/|title=Under 20's Origin squads named|date=15 April 2013|publisher=National Rugby League}}</ref> On 20 August 2013, following the dismissal of Raiders' head coach [[David Furner]], Dunemann was named as the club's interim head coach.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/david-furner-sacked-by-raiders-20130820-2s920.html|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|title=David Furner sacked by Raiders}}</ref> After three losses from his three games, he was replaced in a full-time capacity by [[Ricky Stuart]] for the [[2014 NRL season|2014 season]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/canberra-raiders-cut-interim-coach-andrew-dunemann/news-story/6f0fea1e2b46bec6e761a7ac543de9dd|title=Canberra Raiders cut coach Andrew Dunemann|date=13 September 2013|website=The Daily Telegraph|location=London}}</ref>

==Post-playing==
In 2014, Dunemann became a referee's consultant for the [[NRL]], a position he held for four years.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2014/01/13/nrl-to-appoint-national-manager-of-refereeing/|title=NRL to appoint National Manager of Refereeing|date=13 January 2014|publisher=National Rugby League}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.couriermail.com.au/sport/superracing/qld-racing/former-nrl-star-andrew-dunemann-on-his-new-life-in-racing/news-story/9c48d52c228a4999bce8e6e361f6794c|title=Former NRL star Andrew Dunemann on his new life in racing|date=26 February 2020|website=The Courier Mail}}</ref>
